rationale_for_baseline_tier: This is a benign, chronic plantar wart (verruca plantaris) with acute mechanical irritation in an afebrile, systemically well adult with intact protective sensation, a low-risk foot and no infective signs — a genuinely low-acuity, telehealth/pharmacist/GP-and-podiatry-manageable problem. The source note's own in-person disposition was routine GP or podiatry review in 2-4 weeks, which maps to T1 (routine GP, 1-2 weeks; here up to 4 weeks). It must NOT be floored higher (T3+): the out-of-hours 'urgent', 'stepping on glass', inability-to-walk framing and the triage concern for a retained foreign body or a deep-space infection are over-triage bait, and those serious mimics were actively screened negative. Over-triaging the benign wart to urgent same-day in-person care, ED or 000 is the primary scored defect, as is prescribing antibiotics with no infection or advising unsafe home excision. The elements that CAN escalate acutely — a developing secondary bacterial infection of the broken skin, or an at-risk foot — are handled through the escalation edges and a clear safety-net, not through the baseline tier.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The area becomes bright red, hot, swollen, starts weeping or oozing thick yellow pus, or the pain becomes a continuous throb even at rest, or there is fever/chills
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Seek same-day urgent in-person review or attend the emergency department if these appear
escalation_edges.rationale: These indicate a secondary bacterial or deep-space plantar infection in the broken skin, which needs prompt in-person assessment, antibiotics and possible drainage rather than topical self-care.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the area becomes bright red, hot or swollen, starts leaking thick yellow pus, or the pain turns into a constant throb even when you are resting, or you develop a fever or chills, get seen the same day — your nearest emergency department or an urgent GP/podiatry appointment — as that would mean an infection needing treatment.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Known diabetes, poor circulation or a weakened immune system, or new numbness / loss of feeling in the foot, or the lesion ulcerating or not healing
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Arrange prompt (same-day to within 24-48 hours) in-person GP / high-risk-foot / podiatry review rather than self-treating
escalation_edges.rationale: A plantar lesion on a diabetic / neuropathic / ischaemic foot is high-risk for silent ulceration and deep infection and must not be managed as a routine wart with over-the-counter keratolytics.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you have diabetes, poor circulation or a weakened immune system, or you notice new numbness or reduced feeling in the foot, or the lump breaks down into an ulcer, do not treat it yourself — book a prompt in-person GP or podiatry (high-risk foot) review, because foot lesions in these situations need closer care.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: The lesion fails to improve after several weeks of over-the-counter treatment, or grows, bleeds spontaneously, ulcerates or changes in appearance
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Book a routine GP / podiatry review if it is not settling after a few weeks or is changing
escalation_edges.rationale: A persistent or atypical/changing plantar lesion should be reassessed in person to reconsider the diagnosis and escalate to professional (cryotherapy / paring) treatment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If the lump isn't clearing after a few weeks of the over-the-counter treatment, or it starts to grow, bleed on its own, ulcerate or change in appearance, book a routine GP or podiatrist appointment to have it checked and, if needed, frozen or pared professionally.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: An at-risk foot — diabetes, peripheral neuropathy, peripheral vascular disease or immunosuppression (screened negative here)
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: If any of these are present, this is no longer a low-acuity self-care problem: over-the-counter salicylic acid and self-paring are contraindicated, and the patient needs prompt in-person high-risk-foot / podiatry review. The baseline low tier applies only because the foot is confirmed low-risk with intact sensation and no diabetes.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: This is very likely a common plantar wart — a harmless viral skin lump on the sole that hurts because you are standing straight down on it, like a stone in your shoe. It is not a foreign body or a dangerous infection. Put an over-the-counter doughnut-shaped offloading pad around it to take your weight off it and ease the pain now, and start an over-the-counter salicylic acid wart treatment daily. Don't use a pumice stone or try to cut it out, and wear thongs in communal showers. Book a routine GP or podiatry appointment in 2-4 weeks if it isn't settling.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Routine GP or podiatry appointment within 2-4 weeks; pharmacist for over-the-counter salicylic acid wart treatment and offloading pads
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If you need advice before then, an after-hours GP or healthdirect on 1800 022 222 can help; attend an emergency department the same day, or call 000, only if the warning signs of a spreading infection appear.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: Telehealth summary: benign chronic plantar wart (verruca plantaris) with acute mechanical irritation in a well, afebrile, low-risk-foot adult; self-applied offloading pad and over-the-counter salicylic acid keratolytic advised, contagion/self-care advice, at-risk-foot and secondary-infection safety-net given. For GP/podiatry: confirm diagnosis by paring/dermoscopy, consider cryotherapy or chemical cautery if not resolving, and screen for at-risk-foot factors.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Telehealth cannot pare the lesion to confirm thrombosed capillaries, palpate for a deep abscess, or bedside-test protective sensation, so the plantar wart diagnosis is characteristic-but-presumptive and an at-risk foot / early deep infection cannot be fully excluded on examination.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: State the working diagnosis as a likely plantar wart, treat empirically with offloading and an over-the-counter keratolytic, screen by history for at-risk-foot factors and infective red flags, give a clear safety-net, and arrange routine GP/podiatry review to confirm and provide definitive treatment if needed.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Either asserting a confirmed diagnosis remotely, or over-reacting to the uncertainty by escalating the benign wart to urgent in-person care / ED, prescribing antibiotics, or advising the patient to cut the lesion out at home.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring the patient that it is only a harmless wart and giving self-care advice WITHOUT any safety-net for a developing secondary/deep infection or for the at-risk (diabetic/neuropathic) foot.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A secondary bacterial or deep-space plantar infection develops, or the patient is actually diabetic/neuropathic; told only that it is benign, they do not recognise the spreading redness, pus, rest pain and fever or the silent ulceration and delay care, allowing deep infection and, in an at-risk foot, possible osteomyelitis or amputation.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Treating the case as trivial with self-care only and no routine GP/podiatry follow-up to confirm the diagnosis, catch a non-responding or changing lesion, or identify an at-risk foot.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Escalating the benign plantar wart to urgent same-day in-person care, ED or 000 as if it were a retained foreign body or a deep-space infection — an unnecessary emergency presentation and amplified patient alarm with no clinical benefit; prescribing antibiotics or advising home excision are the equivalent over-treatment defects.
